originalTitle: Hegseth tells Senate war on Iran has so far cost $37.5bn at hearing to request more funds
summary: Defense secretary has asked Senate appropriations committee for nearly $88bn more to continue effort
content: The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth told the Senate on Tuesday that the US-Israel war on Iran had cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members. During an appearance before the Senate appropriations committee, Hegseth, whose opening remarks were repeatedly interrupted by protesters, provided an updated account of the war’s cost – up from previous estimates – and defended the Trump administration’s roughly $88bn supplemental funding request, calling it “urgent, necessary”. Alongside the chair of the joint chiefs, Gen Dan Caine, Hegseth argued that the Pentagon was running low on cash to keep the fight going. Of the total request, as much as $67.1bn is earmarked specifically to cover the costs of the Iran war effort. “Without these funds, we face critical shortfalls,” Hegseth told the Senate panel, warning that it would threaten the defense department’s ability to “pay our service members, rapidly replenish equipment and munitions, and sustain vital operations without disruption”. It was the defense secretary’s first sustained public questioning since a brief ceasefire collapsed and the war roared back to life. At least two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack on Friday, and another died in Iraq destroying an Iranian drone, pushing the American death toll since February to 17. Nearly 100 troops have been wounded in the conflict, officials confirm, though most injuries were minor. Patty Murray, the committee’s Democratic vice-chair, demanded Hegseth explain what the war had actually achieved, accusing the administration of shifting its rationale by the week and warning the conflict risked becoming “another forever war”. “We hear a different rationale for this war every few days,” Murray said in her opening remarks, noting the justification had moved from pre-empting threats that “did not exist” to regime change, then to dismantling Iran’s nuclear programme and navy, and now, she said, to simply reopening the strait of Hormuz, a waterway that was “fully open, toll-free, before this war began”. “Here we are, five months later”, she continued. “The strait is closed. The ceasefire hasn’t just disintegrated – this war is now spiraling out of control again.” Under questioning from lawmakers, Hegseth cast the funding request as the price of deterrence rather than admitting failure, and framed the Pentagon’s overhaul under the US president as a break from what he described as years of slow bureaucracy. “The best way to create peace is to prepare for war, to deter it,” Hegseth told the panel, arguing the department was “building a force so capable that it would be absurd for anyone to challenge it”. Hegseth said the $21bn readiness portion of the request would cover military pay, replace equipment used in recent operations and stabilize fuel shortfalls, while the $46bn capability portion would expand production of “solid rocket motors, Jdams [joint direct attack munitions], hypersonics and counter-drone capabilities”. Without the funds, he warned, the department faced “critical shortfalls” that threaten its ability to pay troops and replenish munitions, and he cautioned that failing to fund the Pentagon at $1.5tn was “the greatest threat that our nation faces”. Some Democrats, including Chris Coons of Delaware, plan to direct criticism at Russell Vought, director of office of management and budget, who is not testifying but who earlier told US Congress that the war’s running cost was about $30bn, a figure that is largely believed by experts to be a severe undercount. Hegseth and Caine faced potential threats from within their own party. Susan Collins of Maine, chair of the committee, and Alaska’s Lisa Murkowski, two prominent Republicans, have both crossed the aisle to back war-powers resolutions aimed at reining in the president’s authority over the conflict. Collins, who is facing a competitive re-election contest, has previously voiced frustration that the administration’s strategy for the war seems to shift almost daily. During the hearing, she asked Hegseth about the effect on military exercises if the supplemental funding is not provided. The defense secretary said that current and future training “would have to be curtailed” if the Pentagon does not get its budget needs met urgently. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican of Louisiana, pressed the panelists on the situation in the strait of Hormuz, asking if Iran will impose tolls on commercial vessels passing through the vital waterway which carried about a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. “That’s a hypothetical question,” Caine replied, “and I don’t know the answer to that.” “Guys, we need straight answers,” Kennedy shot back. Both congressional chambers have passed a war-powers resolution urging the president to seek congressional authorization to continue military operations in Iran. Asked why the administration had not yet sought congressional approval for the war, Hegseth said it was the view of his department that “standalone Article II authorities exist for us to conduct this operation on behalf of the defense of the American people”. The defense secretary evaded the lawmaker’s question about whether he would use the approval of any supplemental funding as a “substitute for explicit congressional authorization”. House representatives have separately accused the Pentagon of stonewalling on basic figures: Betty McCollum, the top Democrat on the House defense appropriations panel, said the department has missed a deadline to detail war costs and has not disclosed how many precision munitions remain after the military burned through thousands of them in Iran.
chineseTitle: 赫格斯在听证会上表示，参议院对伊朗的战争迄今为止已花费 375 亿美元，要求提供更多资金
chineseSummary: 国防部长已要求参议院拨款委员会再拨款近 880 亿美元以继续努力
chineseContent: 美国国防部长皮特·赫格斯周二向参议院表示，美以对伊朗的战争迄今为止已造成 375 亿美元的损失，他面对的议员们对这场冲突不断飙升的代价以及上周三名美国军人的死亡感到愤怒。在参议院拨款委员会的一次露面中，赫格斯的开场白多次被抗议者打断，他提供了战争成本的最新说明（高于之前的估计），并为特朗普政府约 880 亿美元的补充资金请求进行了辩护，称其“紧迫、必要”。赫格塞斯与参谋长联席会议主席丹·凯恩将军一起认为，五角大楼缺乏足够的现金来维持战斗。在全部请求中，高达 671 亿美元专门用于支付伊朗战争的费用。赫格斯对参议院小组表示：“如果没有这些资金，我们将面临严重的短缺。”他警告说，这将威胁国防部“向我们的军人支付工资、快速补充设备和弹药以及在不中断的情况下维持重要行动”的能力。这是自短暂停火破裂、战争重新爆发以来，国防部长首次接受持续的公开质询。周五，至少两名美国士兵在伊朗袭击中丧生，另一名士兵在伊拉克摧毁一架伊朗无人机时丧生，使 2 月以来的美国死亡人数增加至 17 人。官员证实，近 100 名士兵在冲突中受伤，但大多数伤势较轻。该委员会的民主党副主席帕蒂·默里要求赫格斯解释这场战争实际取得了什么成果，指责政府每周都在改变其理由，并警告这场冲突有可能成为“另一场永远的战争”。 “我们听到了这种情况的不同理由
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
